instructions: Answer the following questions to evaluate your understanding after completing the experiment.

questions.question: Which clause is used to filter grouped data based on aggregate function results?
options: WHERE
HAVING
FILTER
GROUP FILTER

questions.question: What will the query "SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT department) FROM employees;" return?
options: The number of unique departments
The total number of employees
The number of departments with employees
An error

questions.question: Which of the following queries will find departments with average salary greater than 50000?
options: SELECT department FROM employees WHERE AVG(salary) > 50000 GROUP BY department;
SELECT department FROM employees GROUP BY department HAVING AVG(salary) > 50000;
SELECT department FROM employees WHERE salary > 50000 GROUP BY department;
SELECT department FROM employees GROUP BY department WHERE AVG(salary) > 50000;

questions.question: What is the result of COUNT(*) when all rows have NULL values in a column?
options: COUNT(*) still counts all rows, including NULLs
Returns 0
Returns NULL
Causes an error

questions.question: Which aggregate function can be used with both numeric and non-numeric data types?
options: SUM()
AVG()
COUNT()
All of them

questions.question: In a query with GROUP BY, can you use ORDER BY to sort the grouped results?
options: Yes, ORDER BY can be used after GROUP BY
No, ORDER BY cannot be used with GROUP BY
Only if HAVING is also used
Only with aggregate functions

questions.question: What does the query "SELECT department, MAX(salary), MIN(salary) FROM employees GROUP BY department;" return?
options: Each department with its highest and lowest salary
The overall maximum and minimum salary across all departments
An error because multiple aggregates are used
Only the maximum salary per department

questions.question: Can you use multiple columns in a GROUP BY clause?
options: Yes, you can group by multiple columns
No, only one column is allowed
Only if they are in the same table
Only with HAVING clause

questions.question: What is the difference between COUNT(*) and COUNT(column_name)?
options: There is no difference
COUNT(*) counts all rows, COUNT(column_name) counts non-NULL values
COUNT(*) only counts unique values
COUNT(column_name) counts all rows including NULLs

questions.question: Which of the following is a valid use of aggregate functions?
options: SELECT name, COUNT(*) FROM employees WHERE COUNT(*) > 5;
SELECT department, COUNT(*) FROM employees GROUP BY department HAVING COUNT(*) > 5;
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM employees WHERE AVG(salary) > 50000;
SELECT name, SUM(salary) FROM employees;

questions.question: What happens if you use an aggregate function without GROUP BY on a table with multiple rows?
options: It returns a single row with the aggregate result
It returns an error
It returns one row per table row
It requires GROUP BY to work

questions.question: In the query "SELECT department, AVG(salary) as avg_sal FROM employees GROUP BY department ORDER BY avg_sal DESC;", what does ORDER BY sort by?
options: The average salary in descending order
The department name in descending order
The number of employees in descending order
An error occurs

questions.question: Can you use aggregate functions in the SELECT clause without GROUP BY?
options: Yes, it will aggregate all rows into a single result
No, GROUP BY is always required
Only COUNT() can be used without GROUP BY
Only if HAVING is used

questions.question: What is the purpose of using DISTINCT with COUNT()?
options: To count only unique values in a column
To count all values including duplicates
To exclude NULL values
To sort the results

questions.question: Which query correctly finds the total salary expenditure per department for departments with more than 10 employees?
options: SELECT department, SUM(salary) FROM employees WHERE COUNT(*) > 10 GROUP BY department;
SELECT department, SUM(salary) FROM employees GROUP BY department HAVING COUNT(*) > 10;
SELECT department, SUM(salary) FROM employees GROUP BY department WHERE COUNT(*) > 10;
SELECT department, SUM(salary) FROM employees HAVING COUNT(*) > 10 GROUP BY department;
